opp.name: Kontoor_Opp_Jan'26
opp.next_step: Jun 25, 2026 (due Jun 25, 2026): Sent Alex invitation to Horizon '26. Re-engage per his requst first week of July. 5/11/26 MQ Alex can't attend Charlotte Roundtable as he will be in Asia until end of May. Competing priorities taking precedence currently. Asked for follow up in July. Will re-engage first week of July. 4/1/26 MQ Demo for Alex Ramirez - VP Global Procurement. He mentioned multiple times during the demo that he had seen much of our solution mirrored in others he had used in the past. Complementary but lacked the Wow factor. 3/25/26 MQ Demo rescheduled for 3/31/26 3/20/26 MQ Demo rescheduled for 3/25/26 3/3/26 MQ Demo scheduled for 3/10/26. 2/24/26 MQ Deep dive discovery call scheduled for 3/2/26 with Alex Ramirez - VP Global Procurement. - Marc will share the deck and retail case studies with Alex, which he'd share with his team internally. Then after 2 weeks (as he is travelling), by end of January, wants to reconnect for detailed demo.
opp.ais_why:
• Rare verbatim buyer voice from the 4/1/26 demo, captured by Marc Q in the next-step note: Alex Ramirez, VP Global Procurement, "mentioned multiple times...he had seen much of our solution mirrored in others" and said the offering "lacked the Wow factor". This is direct buyer feedback that AI capabilities did not differentiate. The demo was rescheduled three times and on 5/11 Alex pushed re-engagement to July.
• The product scope is broad - Merlin, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI and ANA all attached, plus iSource, iSave and iRisk - and the Merlin product line covers all four AI offerings. But this is Zycus-side scoping. The buyer reaction was "complimentary but lacked the Wow factor". No Merlin, Agentic or Intake demand has been recorded; the 'Important' AI rating is unsupported by buyer voice.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls at opportunity, account or domain level across kontoorbrands.com and kontoor.com. All demos must be happening off-Avoma. With the deal stalled to July and the buyer on record that the solution lacked the wow factor, there is no AI upgrade path available right now.

sources: SF Next_Step__c (discovery call 02 Mar, demo 01 Apr, re-engagement ask 11 May): no quantified metric recorded
SF Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c: all null or empty
metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem, savings target, cost reduction, cycle-time improvement, or ROI has been articulated by the buyer across any call, Next Step entry, or Salesforce field. The discovery call scheduled for 02 March and the demo on 01 April focused on solution capabilities, but no outcome from those sessions captured a financial or operational metric. Alex Ramirez (VP Global Procurement) has not volunteered pain quantification, and Marc has not recorded one. The 11 May note states 'competing priorities taking precedence,' implying procurement bandwidth constraints, but this is qualitative, not a measurable business driver. Without a quantified value case, commercial conversations and executive sponsorship will remain out of reach. The immediate move is to re-open the conversation in early July (per Alex's request) and anchor it to a specific cost, cycle-time, or compliance metric that procurement leadership cares about, then build the ROI narrative around that anchor.

sources: SF Next_Step__c, 11 May 2026: Alex asked to re-engage in July, citing competing priorities (passive, not advocating)
SF Next_Step__c, 25 Jun 2026: Marc sent Horizon invite; no buyer reply (cooling signal)
SF Next_Step__c, 01 Apr 2026: Alex's demo feedback 'lacked Wow factor' (not enthusiastic)
SF OpportunityContactRole, X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c: no champion identified
champion.narrative: No champion has been identified or developed. Alex Ramirez, the VP Global Procurement and the sole engaged stakeholder, is not exhibiting champion behavior: he has deferred re-engagement twice (11 May: asked to reconnect in July; 25 June: Horizon invite sent, no reply), has not introduced other stakeholders, has not advocated for Zycus internally (no evidence of him sharing materials or driving agenda), and gave lukewarm feedback after the demo ('lacked Wow factor'). His engagement is passive and cooling. Krissy Dixon, the Executive Assistant, is a scheduling contact, not a champion. No one in the contact roles, Next Step history, or tasks has shown access to the Economic Buyer, push-back on competitors for Zycus, or internal advocacy. The X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c and Champion_Identified__c fields are empty. This is a severe gap: without a champion, the deal has no internal sponsor to navigate the org, surface the decision process, or pull Zycus into exec conversations. Alex may become a champion if the July re-engagement succeeds and if Marc can show differentiated value (likely via Merlin AI positioning), but as of today he is a cooling evaluator, not an advocate. The immediate move is to win Alex over by addressing his 'Wow factor' concern with a Merlin-led narrative, then ask him to introduce the Economic Buyer or C-level sponsor.

sources: SF Next_Step__c, 01 Apr 2026: Alex said capabilities 'mirrored in others he had used in the past' (confirms benchmarking, competitor unnamed)
SF Competitors__c, Existing_vendor__c, How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c: null or empty
competition.narrative: The competitive set is unknown. Alex Ramirez said on 01 April that Zycus capabilities were 'mirrored in others he had used in the past,' confirming he is benchmarking against at least one other vendor or prior tool, but he did not name it. The Salesforce fields Competitors__c, Others_Competitors_Please_specify__c, Existing_vendor__c, and How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c are all empty. No Next Step entry, task, or field names a competing vendor (e.g. SAP Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer). The product scope (iSource, iSave, iSupplier, Merlin) and buyer profile (global apparel/retail manufacturer) suggest likely competitors are SAP Ariba (market leader, often incumbent in Fortune 500 procurement) or Coupa (strong in retail), but this is inference, not confirmation. The gap is critical: without knowing who Zycus is being compared against, Marc cannot position the wedge or counter the competitor's narrative. The fact that Alex found the demo undifferentiated ('lacked Wow factor') strongly implies a head-to-head comparison is underway. The first move in the July re-engagement is to ask Alex directly: what other solutions are you evaluating, and what are the top 2-3 things you need to see that would set one vendor apart. Then build the competitive positioning around Merlin AI as the differentiator.

sources: SF Next_Step__c, 01 Apr 2026: Alex's feedback that capabilities 'mirrored in others' and 'lacked Wow factor' (implies dissatisfaction with status quo)
SF Products__c: Merlin, Agentic AI, ANA in scope (inferred pain: manual processes, need for automation)
SF Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c: null or empty (pain not quantified or formalized)
identify_pain.narrative: The specific operational or financial pain driving this evaluation has not been articulated by the buyer in quantified terms, but qualitative pain is inferred from context. Alex Ramirez requested a 'deep dive discovery call' and a demo, signaling dissatisfaction with the current procurement stack or a gap in capabilities (likely around AI/automation, given the Merlin and Agentic AI products in scope). His 01 April comment that Zycus capabilities were 'mirrored in others he had used in the past' implies he has prior experience with legacy or competing tools and is seeking something materially better. The pain is likely: (1) manual, low-ROI procurement processes that AI/Merlin could automate (inferred from product scope), and (2) lack of differentiation or innovation in the current stack (confirmed by his 'lacked Wow factor' feedback). However, Alex has not named the current vendor (Existing_vendor__c and Replacing_What__c are empty), has not quantified the cost of the pain (no savings target, no error rate, no cycle-time baseline), and has not stated what happens if this is not solved (What_if_this_is_not_done__c is empty). The pain exists but is under-developed. The move is to ask Alex in July: what is broken today, what is the cost of that breakage, and who owns fixing it.

sources: SF Next_Step__c, tasks, fields: no mention of legal, contracting, InfoSec, or implementation
SF Current_Contract_Expiration__c: null
paper_process.narrative: No contracting, legal, procurement-channel, or implementation-scoping discussion has occurred. The deal has not reached commercials, so MSA, SOW, InfoSec review, IT integration, and partner/SI involvement are all unaddressed. The Current_Contract_Expiration__c field is empty, so there is no known forcing function from an expiring incumbent contract. No task, Next Step entry, or Salesforce field mentions legal review, redlining, procurement ops, or a partner. Alex has not brought up implementation, so it is unknown whether Kontoor would require an SI (e.g. Deloitte, Accenture) or handle deployment internally. This gap is expected at the Formal Evaluation stage, but it becomes a risk if the deal advances without mapping the paper path first. When Marc re-engages in July, he should ask whether Kontoor has standard procurement/contracting requirements (e.g. a preferred MSA template, InfoSec questionnaire, data-residency constraints) so those can be surfaced early and not become late-stage blockers.

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: only Alex Ramirez (VP Global Procurement) and Krissy Dixon (Executive Assistant); no CFO, CPO, or C-level sponsor
SF Next_Step__c: no mention of budget authority, pricing, or commercial discussion across all entries
SF Who_will_approve_budget__c, X10a_Sponsor__c, Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c: null or empty
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer has been identified or engaged after 175 days and a VP-level demo. Alex Ramirez carries the title VP Global Procurement, but his role in this deal has been as an evaluator and gatekeeper, not a budget authority: he requested a demo, attended it, then deferred re-engagement twice (to end of May, then to July) citing competing priorities and travel. He has not brought pricing, commercial terms, or budget into any conversation, and he has not introduced a CFO, CPO, or C-level sponsor. The Salesforce fields Who_will_approve_budget__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c, and X10a_Sponsor__c are all empty. No executive sponsor or budget-holder appears in the contact roles, the Next Step history, or any recorded task. This is a severe gap: without an Economic Buyer mapped and engaged, the deal cannot progress to commercials, and the current Pipeline forecast is indefensible. The first move in the July re-engagement is to ask Alex directly who controls the procurement budget for a quarter-million-dollar SaaS purchase and request an introduction.

sources: SF Next_Step__c: only scheduling and deferral notes; no process or timeline mentioned
SF What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c: null or empty
SF OpportunityContactRole: only 2 contacts, no exec or cross-functional stakeholders
decision_process.narrative: The buyer's decision process is unknown. The deal has run one discovery call (02 March), one demo (01 April, rescheduled twice), and two deferral points (11 May, 25 June), but no structured evaluation timeline, no multi-stage approval chain, and no formal next gate have been articulated. Alex Ramirez is the only engaged stakeholder; no exec, no procurement ops team, no IT/InfoSec, and no finance have joined any conversation, so the org structure and approval flow are invisible. The Next Step entries describe scheduling and postponements, not a process. The Salesforce fields What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c, and Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c are empty. The close date of 16 Dec 2026 implies a timeline exists in someone's mind, but it has not been confirmed with the buyer. The deal is in Formal Evaluation, yet no formal evaluation plan (RFI, RFP, shortlist, finalist demos, reference checks) has been shared. This is a critical knowledge gap that blocks pacing and pipeline confidence. In the July re-engagement, Marc must ask Alex: what is the evaluation timeline, who else needs to be involved, and what are the gates between now and a decision.

sources: SF Products__c: iSource, iSave, iSupplier, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI, ANA (confirms AI in scope)
SF Next_Step__c, 01 Apr 2026: Alex said capabilities 'mirrored in others' and 'lacked Wow factor' (differentiation is the live criterion)
SF Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c: empty
decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's evaluation criteria are implicit in the product scope (iSource, iSave, iSupplier, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI, ANA) and in Alex's reaction to the 01 April demo, but they have not been formalized or validated in a structured way. Alex said the capabilities he saw were 'mirrored in others he had used in the past' and 'lacked the Wow factor,' signaling that he is benchmarking Zycus against prior tools (likely SAP Ariba, Coupa, or another incumbent in his stack) and that differentiation is the unmet criterion. The Products__c field lists Merlin and Agentic AI, indicating AI/automation is in scope, but the demo did not land the AI wedge strongly enough to create separation. No RFI, RFP, scoring rubric, or formal decision-criteria doc has been shared. The Business_Requirements__c and Top_Challenges_Priorities__c fields are empty. The partial signal is: (1) AI and procurement automation are criteria (inferred from product scope and Merlin inclusion), and (2) differentiation vs incumbents is the live criterion Alex is testing (confirmed by his 01 April feedback). The gap is: we do not know the weightings, the other vendors in the comparison set, or whether there is a formal scorecard. The next step is to ask Alex in July what the top 3 decision factors are and how Zycus ranks on each.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit is inferred but not confirmed. The product scope includes Merlin, Agentic AI, and ANA, indicating the buyer requested or was offered AI/automation capabilities, which suggests latent AI appetite. However, AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, and AIS_Why__c are all null, so no formal AI excitement signal was logged. Alex's 01 April feedback ('lacked Wow factor') suggests he did not see Merlin or AI as differentiated, which could mean either (1) he is AI Curious but not convinced Zycus delivers superior AI, or (2) he is AI Resistant and views AI as table stakes, not a wedge. The absence of an enthusiastic AI reaction in any call or field, combined with the product scope, places this buyer in the AI Curious tier: interested enough to include AI products in scope, but not yet sold on the value. The move is to re-engage with a quantified Merlin ROI story (e.g. negotiation-time savings, contract-value capture, intake automation headcount reduction) to convert curiosity into hunger.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 07 Jan 2026. Discovery call scheduled 24 Feb, held 02 Mar. Demo scheduled 03 Mar for 10 Mar, rescheduled twice (20 Mar to 25 Mar, 25 Mar to 31 Mar), held 01 Apr. Alex gave lukewarm feedback (capabilities 'mirrored in others,' 'lacked Wow factor'). No activity until 11 May, when Alex declined Charlotte Roundtable (traveling Asia until end of May, competing priorities), asked to re-engage in July. Marc sent Horizon invite 25 Jun, awaiting reply. Last two-way buyer touch: 11 May (51 days ago). Deal has been in early-stage demo/discovery mode for entire 175-day lifecycle with no progression to commercials, multi-threading, or executive engagement.

champion_strength.source: SF Next_Step__c (01 Apr, 11 May, 25 Jun), SF OpportunityContactRole
champion_strength.summary: No champion identified. Alex Ramirez, the only engaged stakeholder, is not exhibiting champion behavior: he has deferred twice, gave lukewarm demo feedback, has not introduced other stakeholders or the Economic Buyer, and has not advocated for Zycus internally. His engagement is passive and cooling. Without a champion, the deal has no internal sponsor to navigate the org, surface the decision process, or pull Zycus into exec conversations.

items.commitment: Share demo deck and retail case studies with the relevant stakeholder for internal distribution
Alex Ramirez to confirm July re-engagement date and agenda

items.action: Re-engage the relevant stakeholder in the first week of July per his request. Frame the conversation around quantified ROI and position Merlin AI (negotiation agent, intake automation, I2O agentic workflows) as the differentiation wedge that addresses his 'lacked Wow factor' concern from the 01 April demo. Ask three critical questions: (1) what are the top 3 evaluation criteria and how does Zycus rank, (2) what other vendors are in the mix, and (3) who controls the procurement budget for this purchase. Secure a confirmed next step with a date (ideally a follow-on call with the Economic Buyer or a commercial discussion) before ending the call.
items.trigger: Alex asked to re-engage in July on 11 May; Marc sent Horizon invite 25 Jun awaiting reply. Last two-way touch 51 days ago.
items.expected_effect: Restart momentum, test whether buyer interest is recoverable, surface Economic Buyer and competitive set, move to commercials if Alex re-engages positively. If Alex does not respond or defers again, this is a qualification-out signal.

items.action: Request introduction to the Economic Buyer (CFO, CPO, or C-level sponsor who controls the procurement budget). Alex has been the sole contact for 175 days with no budget or commercial discussion. Explain that to progress to pricing and ROI validation, Zycus needs to understand the business case and decision authority. If Alex cannot or will not introduce the EB, this is a red flag that he lacks internal influence or that the deal is not real.
items.trigger: No Economic Buyer identified after 175 days. No pricing, budget, or commercial conversation in any field or activity.
items.expected_effect: Map the budget authority and decision-maker, create exec-level sponsorship, unlock path to commercials. If request is blocked, this signals weak champion and low deal probability.

items.action: Multi-thread to at least two additional stakeholders: procurement ops (the team that would use iSource/iSave/iSupplier day-to-day) and IT/InfoSec (integration and security requirements). Ask Alex to introduce them for a brief use-case or technical alignment call. If Alex resists or says he will handle it internally, push back gently: 'We've found that early alignment with the end users and IT prevents late-stage blockers. Can we set up a 30-minute session this month?' Single-threading to a cooling VP is a deal-killer.
items.trigger: Only 2 contacts (Alex Ramirez, Krissy Dixon) in contact roles after 175 days. No procurement ops, IT, or cross-functional engagement.
items.expected_effect: Reduce single-thread risk, surface additional champions or detractors, create multiple paths of communication so the deal does not die if Alex goes dark again.

items.action: Run a competitive discovery conversation. Ask Alex: 'You mentioned in April that some of our capabilities reminded you of tools you've used before. Can you share which solutions you're comparing us against and what the top 2-3 differentiators need to be for you?' Use his answer to position Merlin AI as the wedge (negotiation agent on supplier price history, agentic intake workflows, AI-driven sourcing recommendations). If he names SAP Ariba or Coupa, prepare a side-by-side on AI/automation depth where Zycus leads.
items.trigger: Alex said on 01 Apr that capabilities were 'mirrored in others he had used in the past' and 'lacked Wow factor,' confirming benchmarking. Competitors__c and related fields are empty.
items.expected_effect: Surface the competitive set, understand where Zycus stands, tailor positioning to win on differentiation (AI/Merlin as wedge). If Alex will not share competitors, this signals he is managing a controlled bake-off and Zycus may be a price anchor, not a finalist.

items.action: Executive connect: if the July re-engagement with Alex succeeds and he introduces the Economic Buyer or commits to a commercial discussion, bring the deal owner's manager into the next call to demonstrate senior-level commitment and to build rapport with the buyer's exec. If Alex does not re-engage or defers again by mid-July, the deal owner's manager should make a direct outreach to Alex (LinkedIn message or email) to test whether there is any path forward or whether this should be qualified out.
items.trigger: Deal at severe risk: 51 days since last buyer touch, two deferrals, cooling contact, no exec engagement. Executive touch may unlock sponsorship or confirm deal is lost.
items.expected_effect: If buyer re-engages, exec presence signals seriousness and can unlock C-level introductions. If buyer does not respond to exec outreach, this is a qualification-out signal and the deal should be moved to the relevant stakeholder or Omitted to clean the pipeline.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling. Last two-way buyer touch was 11 May (51 days ago), when Alex deferred to July citing competing priorities. Rep reached out 25 Jun with Horizon invite; no buyer reply. Deal cadence has decayed: demo in April, silence until May check-in, then another 6-week gap. Alex's lukewarm demo feedback ('lacked Wow factor') and repeated deferrals signal waning interest. No multi-threading, no exec pull-in, no commercial momentum. Severe ghost risk if July re-engagement does not produce a confirmed next step with a date.

north_star_verdict.math: Days to close: 168 (close 2026-12-16). Created 07 Jan 2026, now 175 days old. Time in Formal Evaluation: unknown submission date for this stage, but deal has been in early-stage evaluation mode (demos and discovery) for the entire 175-day lifecycle with no progression signal. Last two-way buyer engagement: 11 May 2026 (51 days ago), when Alex declined the Charlotte Roundtable and asked to re-engage in July. Rep-initiated outreach on 25 June (Horizon invite) is unconfirmed; no buyer reply recorded. Forward slip: none recorded in field history. Pace required to close by 16 Dec: the deal must move from Formal Evaluation through Shortlisted, Vendor Selected, Negotiation, and Contract in 168 days, but it has been stuck in early discovery/demo for 175 days with cooling engagement, no executive access, no commercial traction, and no confirmed next step. The 168-day runway is arithmetically sufficient, but the deal dynamics (single-thread, cooling contact, no differentiation, no EB/DM, no budget conversation) make progression highly unlikely without immediate intervention.
evidence: Last two-way buyer touch: 11 May 2026 (51 days ago), when Alex Ramirez declined Charlotte event and asked to reconnect in July (SF Next_Step__c).

Demo on 1 April 2026: Alex said Zycus capabilities 'mirrored in others he had used in the past' and 'lacked the Wow factor' (SF Next_Step__c).
No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or executive sponsor in contact roles or engagement history after 175 days (SF OpportunityContactRole, SF Next_Step__c).
No commercial, pricing, or ROI discussion recorded in any field or activity (SF Next_Step__c, SF tasks).

north_star_verdict.headline: Single-threaded to a cooling VP-level stakeholder who has not responded to two re-engagement attempts (11 May, 25 June). No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or executive sponsor identified after 175 days. Demo lacked differentiation (Alex said capabilities 'mirrored in others' and 'lacked Wow factor' on 1 April). No commercial discussion, no ROI workshop, no champion. Competing priorities at the buyer have taken precedence. Rep reached out 25 June with Horizon invite; awaiting July callback but momentum is at severe risk.

Merlin AI is in scope but was not positioned as the wedge. Re-engage with a dedicated Merlin/AI value session (negotiation agent savings use case, intake automation ROI) tied to a quantified pain. Bring a reference customer or proof point that shows unique AI impact competitors cannot match.
Single-threaded: only Alex Ramirez (VP Global Procurement) engaged; no multi-threading to exec, procurement ops, IT, or finance after 175 days.
No Economic Buyer or Decision Maker identified or engaged. Alex has not discussed budget, pricing, or commercial terms and has not introduced a CFO, CPO, or C-level sponsor.
No champion. Alex is cooling (deferred twice, lukewarm demo feedback, no reply to 25 Jun outreach) and has shown no internal advocacy.
Demo lacked differentiation: Alex said on 01 Apr that capabilities were 'mirrored in others' and 'lacked Wow factor.' Merlin AI wedge not landed.
No quantified business case, savings target, or ROI articulated by buyer.
Competitive set unknown. Alex confirmed benchmarking but did not name competitor.
Last two-way buyer touch 51 days ago (11 May). Rep reached out 25 Jun; awaiting reply.
best_practice_check.summary: Multiple critical best-practice violations after 175 days: single-threaded to one VP-level contact who is cooling; no Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or executive sponsor identified or engaged; no champion; no quantified business case or ROI; demo lacked differentiation (Alex: 'lacked Wow factor'); competitive set unknown despite confirmed benchmarking; no commercial or pricing discussion; last two-way buyer touch 51 days ago (11 May). Deal is in Formal Evaluation but has not progressed beyond early discovery/demo in nearly 6 months.

competitive_position.summary: Competitive set is unknown. Alex Ramirez confirmed on 01 April that he is benchmarking Zycus against at least one other vendor or prior tool ('capabilities mirrored in others he had used in the past'), but he did not name it. His feedback that the demo 'lacked the Wow factor' signals Zycus is not differentiated in his eyes. Likely competitors (inferred, not confirmed): SAP Ariba (market leader, common incumbent in Fortune 500 procurement) or Coupa (strong in retail/apparel). The wedge is unpositioned: Merlin AI and Agentic AI are in the product scope, but the demo did not land them as a differentiator. Winning depends on re-opening the conversation in July with a Merlin-led narrative (negotiation agent, intake automation, AI-driven sourcing) and asking Alex directly what other vendors are in the mix and what would set one apart.

competitors.name: Unknown incumbents or alternatives
competitors.quote: He mentioned multiple times during the demo that he had seen much of our solution mirrored in others he had used in the past. Complementary but lacked the Wow factor.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is weak. Merlin, Agentic AI, and ANA are in the product scope, signaling AI is relevant to the buyer's needs, but the 01 April demo did not land the AI wedge: Alex said capabilities were 'mirrored in others' and 'lacked Wow factor,' meaning the differentiation that Merlin should provide was not communicated effectively. No Salesforce field or Next Step entry records a Merlin-specific conversation, ROI narrative, or use-case demo. The AI products are listed but not positioned as the wedge. This is a severe under-positioning risk: if the buyer is evaluating modern procurement platforms, AI/automation is the battleground, and Zycus has a strong story (Merlin negotiation agent, intake automation, I2O agentic workflows) that was not told. Recovering this deal depends on re-opening the July conversation with a Merlin-led narrative.

items.quote: He mentioned multiple times during the demo that he had seen much of our solution mirrored in others he had used in the past. Complementary but lacked the Wow factor.
items.source: SF Next_Step__c, demo feedback from Alex Ramirez
items.criterion: Differentiation vs prior tools and competitors

items.quote: Products in scope: iSource, iSave, iSupplier, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI, ANA
items.criterion: AI and automation capabilities (Merlin, Agentic AI)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Fit is at risk. Alex requested a demo and engaged through April, but his 01 April feedback ('capabilities mirrored in others,' 'lacked Wow factor') signals Zycus did not meet his expectation for differentiation. He is benchmarking against prior tools or active competitors and found the demo unremarkable. His subsequent deferrals (11 May, 25 June) and competing-priorities comment indicate procurement bandwidth is constrained and Zycus is not top-of-mind. The product scope (Merlin, Agentic AI) aligns with modern procurement AI needs, but the value prop was not landed effectively. Fit can be recovered if the July re-engagement reframes the conversation around quantified ROI and Merlin AI as the wedge.

hard.ais_why:
• Rare verbatim buyer voice from the 4/1/26 demo, captured by Marc Q in the next-step note: Alex Ramirez, VP Global Procurement, "mentioned multiple times...he had seen much of our solution mirrored in others" and said the offering "lacked the Wow factor". This is direct buyer feedback that AI capabilities did not differentiate. The demo was rescheduled three times and on 5/11 Alex pushed re-engagement to July.
• The product scope is broad - Merlin, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI and ANA all attached, plus iSource, iSave and iRisk - and the Merlin product line covers all four AI offerings. But this is Zycus-side scoping. The buyer reaction was "complimentary but lacked the Wow factor". No Merlin, Agentic or Intake demand has been recorded; the 'Important' AI rating is unsupported by buyer voice.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls at opportunity, account or domain level across kontoorbrands.com and kontoor.com. All demos must be happening off-Avoma. With the deal stalled to July and the buyer on record that the solution lacked the wow factor, there is no AI upgrade path available right now.
